objections.text: We cannot justify the 50k annual investment without clear evidence of a 12-month payback period on our specific workflows
Integration with our legacy MLS system may require custom development that we have not budgeted for
The reporting dashboard looks promising but we need proof it will reduce our data analysis time by at least 25 percent
Our CFO has directed us to halt new SaaS purchases until Q3 due to budget reallocation constraints

channel.name: deal-propelview
channel.topic: PropelView Analytics - Real Estate Tech - $50k ARR

messages.body: Just wrapped discovery with PropelView. David Kapoor is the finance guy - he's going to be our real gatekeeper here. Mentioned their MLS integration is consuming 20-30 hours a week in manual work.
That's solid. Did they give you a sense of what they'd need to move forward? Budget constraints, timeline, anything?

David's pushing hard for 12-month payback evidence. Specific to their workflows. Sarah Mitchell - that's the ops person - thinks they could defer a new hire if this works. We're doing a technical deep dive early April.
Got it. I'll prep a custom ROI model based on their labor costs. We can build that out before the tech call. How much time are they spending on data analysis specifically?
Demo went okay but there's some teeth in this. Marcus Thompson - the tech lead - wants proof on the 25 percent efficiency gain claim. Sarah wasn't thrilled about data refresh frequency either.

Yeah, refresh frequency is standard - we update every 4 hours. Let me send them the compliance docs on MLS data security. That was one of Jennifer Walsh's concerns too. How did the dashboard land otherwise?
Visually they liked it. But David is the blocker - said CFO has a freeze on new SaaS through Q3. That's real. We need to solve for that.
Q3 freeze is tough but not impossible. Can we defer start and billing? Lock pricing now, activate in July?
That's what I proposed to David on the 12th. He seemed open but needs CFO sign-off. Said if we can run a POC on their actual data and prove the 25 percent gain, that helps the business case.
Marcus is on board with the POC. I walked through the MLS integration with them on the 28th - no custom dev needed, which kills one of their objections. We're running the POC this week.

subject: Introducing InsightModal - Real Estate Analytics Platform for PropelView
body_summary: Initial outreach highlighting InsightModal capabilities for real estate technology companies
body: Hi Sarah, I came across PropelView while researching how mid-market real estate tech companies are handling data analysis workflows, and your platform stood out. With your Tableau setup and AWS infrastructure, you're clearly committed to data-driven insights for your clients. The challenge I keep hearing from teams like yours is that legacy MLS integrations create bottlenecks in reporting cycles, eating into time your analysts could spend on strategy. InsightModal was built specifically for real estate tech workflows. We help companies reduce data analysis cycles by standardizing how property datasets flow through analytics pipelines, without requiring custom development on your MLS connections. Given PropelView's 150-300 person scale and the complexity of your current setup, there's likely 15-20 hours per month in efficiency gains just waiting to be unlocked. Would it make sense to grab 20 minutes next week so I can show you how this works in context of your specific data architecture? Happy to work around your schedule. Best, Michael Chen, InsightModal

subject: Re: Introducing InsightModal - Real Estate Analytics Platform for PropelView
body_summary: Sarah Mitchell expresses initial interest and asks about use cases specific to real estate operations
body: Hi Michael, thanks for reaching out about InsightModal. I appreciate you taking the time to introduce the platform. A few quick questions before we explore further: we currently manage property data across multiple MLS feeds and use Tableau for reporting. Our operations team spends considerable time pulling and reconciling data manually each week. How does InsightModal handle integration with legacy MLS systems like ours? Also, given our current tech stack with Salesforce and AWS, what's the implementation timeline typically look like? I'd want to understand the effort required on our end before discussing with our technology team. Can you share some specific examples of how this has worked for similar mid-market real estate firms? Happy to set up a brief call next week if you have availability.

subject: Re: Introducing InsightModal - Real Estate Analytics Platform for PropelView
body_summary: Michael proposes discovery call for March 26 to explore PropelView specific analytics needs
body:
Sarah, great questions - and I appreciate you laying out your specific situation with the MLS feeds and manual reconciliation work. That's exactly where we see the biggest wins with mid-market real estate firms like yours.

On integration: our MLS connector works natively with legacy systems through standard data feeds. No custom development required for most implementations. I'll send over technical specs, but the short version is we typically integrate with existing Salesforce and Tableau stacks within 2-3 weeks of kickoff.

I worked with Cornerstone Properties in Dallas last year - similar setup to yours with multiple MLS sources. They were doing 8-10 hours weekly on data reconciliation. After implementation, that dropped to about 2 hours for exception handling.

I'd love to explore whether InsightModal could make sense for PropelView. How does Tuesday March 26th at 2pm PT work for a 20-minute discovery call? I'll send a calendar invite. We can walk through your specific workflows and I can answer any technical questions before you loop in Jennifer.

Looking forward to it.

Michael

title: Initial Discovery Call - PropelView Analytics
transcript:
Michael Chen: Good morning Sarah and David. Thanks for taking the time today. I know you're both busy, so I want to make sure we spend our thirty minutes wisely. From our emails, it sounds like PropelView is looking at ways to streamline your data analysis workflows. Is that a fair assessment?

Sarah Mitchell: Yes, that's right. Our team spends a lot of time pulling data from multiple sources and then manually reconciling it for our reports. It's a bottleneck, especially as we scale.

Michael Chen: That's exactly what we hear from other mid-market real estate tech companies. Before we dive into InsightModal, I'd like to understand your current setup better. You mentioned using Salesforce and Tableau. Walk me through how your team currently handles MLS data integration and reporting.

David Kapoor: We pull data weekly from our legacy MLS system into Salesforce, then we have a team member who spends hours each week manually validating and transforming it in Tableau. It's workable but not efficient.

Michael Chen: And how many hours per week are we talking about here?

Sarah Mitchell: Roughly twenty to thirty hours across two team members. Plus there's always lag time waiting for validation before reports go out to stakeholders.

Michael Chen: That's significant. Now, David, I'm curious about the budget side. When you think about solving this problem, what does the investment need to look like for PropelView to move forward?

David Kapoor: That's the key question for us. We're looking at any solution needing to demonstrate clear ROI. We can't justify fifty thousand a year without seeing concrete evidence of a twelve-month payback period specific to our workflows. Right now I need to see how this actually reduces our costs or frees up capacity in a measurable way.

Michael Chen: Totally fair. That's exactly the conversation we should be having. Most of our customers in your space see payback in the eight to fourteen month range through labor efficiency gains. Sarah, from an operations perspective, if you could free up fifteen hours per week of your team's time, what would that capacity shift look like for you?

Sarah Mitchell: That would be significant. We could redirect that time to more strategic analysis instead of data wrangling. We're already considering hiring another analyst, so if this tool could defer that hire, it would absolutely justify the investment.

David Kapoor: But we need to see it in writing. And we need to understand the implementation burden too. How much of our team's time would InsightModal require to get up and running?

Michael Chen: Good question. With your current Salesforce setup, we're talking five to eight hours of configuration work. We handle the heavy lifting. I'd like to walk you through a fifteen-minute demo focused specifically on MLS integration and the reporting dashboard. But first, can you tell me what success looks like in the first ninety days if we did move forward?

Sarah Mitchell: We need the dashboard operational and pulling clean data automatically. And we need to validate that our manual processing time is actually reduced by at least twenty-five percent in that window.

David Kapoor: Agreed. And then we'd need a clear path to seeing the full ROI case developed for our budget process.

Michael Chen: Perfect. That's our next step then. Jessica and I will set up a technical deep dive to map our standard MLS integration against your legacy system, and we'll build a preliminary ROI model based on your specific labor costs and timeline. Does the week of April first work for a follow-up technical call?

Sarah Mitchell: Yes, that works for us.

David Kapoor: Works here too. Send over the calendar invite.
summary: Sarah Mitchell and David Kapoor outlined PropelView's current data integration bottleneck consuming twenty to thirty hours weekly. David raised a critical ROI objection requiring twelve-month payback evidence specific to their workflows, while Sarah identified potential capacity gains that could defer a new hire. The team agreed to schedule a technical deep dive for early April to map MLS integration details and develop a preliminary ROI model based on PropelView's specific labor costs.
objections_raised: We cannot justify the 50k annual investment without clear evidence of a 12-month payback period on our specific workflows
next_steps: Schedule technical deep dive with Jessica Ramirez for week of April 1st to validate MLS legacy system integration
Develop preliminary ROI model based on PropelView's labor costs and demonstrated twenty to thirty hour weekly time commitment
Prepare fifteen-minute dashboard demo focused on automatic MLS data integration and reporting capabilities

note_preview: Initial discovery call completed. Strong operations interest identified.
author: Michael Chen
content: Discovery call with Sarah Mitchell and Marcus Thompson went well on the operations side but David Kapoor is already pushing back on the 50k annual investment. Sarah clearly sees the value in our reporting dashboard for her workflows but we need a strong ROI case to get David to move forward. Getting Marcus involved is good because he understands the process optimization angle but he's going to want proof on the 25 percent time savings claim before he'll support it.

note_preview: Sarah Mitchell identified as champion. Director of Operations advocates solution.
author: Michael Chen
content: Sarah Mitchell is clearly our champion here. She gets the value prop immediately and sees how this solves their reporting bottleneck in ops. Director level authority plus genuine enthusiasm for the tool puts us in a strong position if we can navigate David's budget concerns.

subject: Follow-up: Discovery Call Insights and Next Steps
body_summary: Summary of discovery call covering current analytics workflows and identified pain points
body: Sarah, thanks again for the time this morning. I appreciated hearing about PropelView's current Tableau setup and the specific challenges your team faces with MLS data consolidation across multiple markets. The manual reconciliation process you described is clearly a bottleneck. I wanted to follow up on a couple of points from our call, particularly around your 12-month payback requirement. I understand that's a critical metric for any new investment, and frankly, it's one where we need to align on exactly what ROI looks like for your workflows before we move forward. Rather than making assumptions, I'd like Jessica and I to dive deeper into your specific data volumes and analyst hours spent on current processes. That's the only way we can give you credible numbers on what a 25 percent reduction in analysis time actually means in terms of annual savings. Would next week work for a more technical deep-dive with Jennifer? We can walk through a sample integration scenario using your actual data structure. Looking forward to tackling this together.

note_preview: Objection raised: CFO requires 12-month payback period justification needed.
author: Michael Chen
content: David Kapoor pushed back hard on the 50k annual investment cost, stating that his CFO won't approve anything without a clear 12-month payback period specific to their workflows. This is a deal blocker unless we can model out concrete ROI numbers tied to their actual data analysis processes. Need to schedule a follow-up with Sarah Mitchell to understand their current efficiency gaps before we can credibly show them the payback math.

subject: Re: Follow-up: Discovery Call Insights and Next Steps
body_summary: David Kapoor raises concern about ROI justification and 12-month payback period requirement
body: Michael, I appreciate you taking the time to understand our position on this. I need to be direct though - we cannot justify a 50k annual investment without clear evidence of a 12-month payback period on our specific workflows. That's not negotiable on my end. Our board has been scrutinizing every SaaS addition, and frankly, the analysis time reduction you mentioned feels optimistic given our legacy MLS system constraints. Before we schedule another call with Jennifer, I need to see concrete numbers. What does a 25 percent efficiency gain actually translate to in dollars for a shop our size running three concurrent market analyses? Can you walk me through a real scenario - not a case study, but our actual data volumes and how your platform handles our specific reconciliation pain points? If the math doesn't work, we should both know that now rather than after more meetings. If it does, then Jennifer and I can have a productive technical discussion. Send over what you have and let's see if we're aligned on the fundamentals first.

subject: Re: Follow-up: Discovery Call Insights and Next Steps
body_summary: Jessica provides ROI analysis and case study data for similar mid-market real estate clients
body: Hi David, thanks for your follow-up on the financial requirements. I wanted to share some concrete data that directly addresses your 12-month payback concern. We analyzed three mid-market real estate tech companies with similar ARR profiles to PropelView, and they saw an average of 18-22 percent reduction in manual data processing time within the first quarter. For your current team structure, that translates to approximately 240-300 hours recovered annually, which at your loaded cost basis represents roughly 35-40k in labor value recapture. Beyond the direct time savings, our clients report a 12-15 percent improvement in reporting accuracy, reducing compliance and audit costs. I've attached detailed ROI models based on your specific team size and current workflows as discussed. The 50k annual investment typically reaches break-even by month 9-10 for organizations at your scale. Happy to walk through the financial model with you and Sarah in more detail. When works best for you both?

messages.body: PropelView is at risk. CFO freeze on SaaS through Q3. David Kapoor is the gatekeeper and he's serious about it.
Do we have exec sponsor there? Can we escalate above the CFO or is that a waste of time?
Sarah Mitchell is operations, she gets the value. But David's the one talking to CFO. He's not saying no though - he's saying the timing is the issue. We pitched the deferred-start model.
That could work if the POC validates the ROI. How confident is Jessica on hitting that 25 percent benchmark?
She flagged that as aggressive but doable on their specific workflows. The real risk is if the POC doesn't deliver and David uses it as cover to say no.
Understood. Keep me posted on the POC results. If it hits 25 percent, we should be in good shape with the July start. If it doesn't, we need a backup plan.
POC is running. Early data looks positive on the analysis time reduction but I'm not going to oversell it. Should have real numbers by end of May.
Just talked to Sarah. POC is tracking well. She's confident on the business case now. David still waiting on CFO but said the deferred-start structure helps a lot.
Good. That's progress. Is David committed to pushing it through procurement once he gets CFO approval?
He said procurement confirmation pending but he's on it. Sent the formal deferred-start proposal on the 21st. Waiting to hear back but feels like we're in decent shape now.

title: Product Demo - Dashboard and Reporting Features
transcript:
Michael Chen: Good afternoon everyone. Thanks for joining. Today we want to walk through our dashboard and reporting capabilities, focusing on how they can reduce your data analysis cycles. I know from our discovery call that reporting efficiency is a key priority for PropelView. Sarah, we're excited to show you what we've built. Let me start by sharing my screen. Jennifer, Marcus, please jump in with any technical questions as we go.

Sarah Mitchell: Thanks Michael. Yes, we're interested to see how this maps to our current workflows. Our teams spend a lot of time pulling data from multiple sources and manually consolidating it. If we can automate that, it would be valuable.

Michael Chen: Exactly. So here's the core dashboard. You can see we pull data from Salesforce, your MLS feeds, and AWS data warehouses in real time. All your metrics in one place. Marcus, I'm bringing up the reporting module now. You can create custom reports without touching code.

Marcus Thompson: Okay, that's interesting. But I need to understand the data refresh cadence. If I'm running reports for decision making, I need to know the data is current. What's your refresh frequency?

Jessica Ramirez: Great question. Standard is four-hour refresh cycles, but we can configure it to two hours or even real time with higher tier plans. For real estate workflows, most clients find four hours sufficient for daily operations.

Jennifer Walsh: What about security and compliance? We're handling sensitive MLS data. I'll need to validate your encryption standards and audit logging before we commit to anything. That's non-negotiable.

Michael Chen: Absolutely. Jessica, can you speak to that?

Jessica Ramirez: Of course. We're SOC 2 Type II certified. We use AES-256 encryption at rest and TLS 1.3 in transit. All access is logged with immutable audit trails. I can share our security documentation with your team. We also support HIPAA and GDPR if that's relevant.

Jennifer Walsh: Good. I'll need to review that separately. But before I commit my team's time on integration validation, I need to understand the resource requirements and any custom development needs for our legacy MLS system.

Michael Chen: That's a fair point. Our standard MLS connectors cover most systems, but I hear you on legacy concerns. Let's do a technical deep dive next week. Jessica and I can review your specific MLS architecture and identify any gaps.

Sarah Mitchell: I'm also thinking about the ROI side. Michael, in our discovery call, you mentioned we'd see efficiency gains. Marcus, you raised the point about needing proof we'll reduce analysis time by at least 25 percent. Can you show us some benchmarks from similar real estate companies?

Michael Chen: Sarah, that's the right question. I have case studies from three mid-market real estate tech firms. On average, clients see 22 to 28 percent reduction in manual reporting time within six months. I'll send those over. However, the exact number depends on your current tooling and processes. That's why the technical discovery and workflow mapping are critical next steps.

Marcus Thompson: So you're saying 25 percent isn't guaranteed?

Michael Chen: I want to be honest. Based on your current setup with Tableau and AWS, I'm confident we can hit that. But we should validate your specific workflows first. Once we do that mapping, I'll give you a more precise projection.

Jennifer Walsh: That makes sense. I'd rather have an honest assessment than overpromises. What's the timeline if we decide to move forward?

Jessica Ramirez: We typically need two weeks for technical integration planning, then a four-week implementation window. You could be live in six to eight weeks from contract execution.

Sarah Mitchell: Okay. Michael, send us those case studies. Jennifer, can you do that technical deep dive call next week with Jessica?

Jennifer Walsh: Yes, I'll coordinate with my team. But I want to emphasize this is exploratory. We have concerns about custom development cost and timeline.

Michael Chen: Understood. No pressure. We'll get you the data and honest answers. Sarah, anything else?

Sarah Mitchell: Just the ROI documentation. David Kapoor, our CFO, will want to see clear payback math. Michael, that's going to be critical for moving forward.

Michael Chen: Noted. We'll prepare a detailed ROI model based on the case studies and your estimated workflows. Thanks everyone.
summary: Michael Chen and Jessica Ramirez demonstrated the dashboard and reporting capabilities to Sarah Mitchell, Jennifer Walsh, and Marcus Thompson. Key concerns emerged around data refresh frequency, security compliance for sensitive MLS data, and proof of the claimed 25 percent efficiency gain. Sarah emphasized that ROI documentation will be critical for CFO approval. Next steps include sharing case studies, scheduling a technical deep dive on MLS integration, and preparing a detailed ROI model.
objections_raised: Need proof the reporting dashboard will reduce data analysis time by at least 25 percent
Integration with our legacy MLS system may require custom development
Data refresh frequency and currency requirements for decision-making clarity
Security and compliance validation needed for sensitive MLS data before team commitment
next_steps: Michael Chen to send case studies from three similar mid-market real estate tech companies showing efficiency gains
Schedule technical deep dive between Jessica Ramirez and Jennifer Walsh's team to review MLS system architecture and integration requirements
Michael Chen to prepare detailed ROI model including payback period analysis for CFO David Kapoor review
Jessica Ramirez to share SOC 2 Type II certification and security documentation with Jennifer Walsh

note_preview: Product demo completed. Dashboard features well-received by stakeholders.
author: Michael Chen
content: Ran through the reporting dashboard with Sarah, Jennifer, and Marcus today. The visualization capabilities and data refresh speed impressed them, though Marcus immediately asked about the 25 percent time reduction claim on analysis workflows. Sarah flagged the need for proof of concept on their specific MLS data sets before moving forward.

subject: Product Demo Debrief - Dashboard and Reporting Features
body_summary: Follow-up from product demo with feature overview and Q&A responses
body:
Hi Sarah and Jennifer,

Thanks for taking the time on the demo today. I wanted to follow up on a few key points that came up, particularly around the dashboard reporting capabilities.

Jennifer, I appreciated your question about the 25 percent data analysis time reduction. Based on what we showed you with the automated MLS data pulls and the custom report builder, I believe we can demonstrate that specific metric using PropelView's current workflow. I'd like to set up a brief technical deep-dive to map this against your actual data volumes and reporting frequency.

Sarah, I know the ROI justification remains a priority concern from your team. The dashboard efficiency gains are one piece, but I want to ensure we're quantifying the full value across your operations workflows. Could we schedule time next week to walk through a PropelView-specific ROI model? This would incorporate your team's baseline metrics.

I'm attaching the integration documentation Jennifer requested. No custom development should be required for your MLS setup.

Let me know your availability.

Best,
Jessica

note_preview: Objection: Analysis time reduction needs 25 percent minimum proof requirement.
author: Michael Chen
content: Marcus Thompson pushed back hard on the dashboard reporting claims during the demo today. He needs documented proof that we can reduce their data analysis time by at least 25 percent on their specific workflows before he'll move forward. I need to pull together a detailed ROI analysis tied to their current process and get Jessica to run a custom benchmark against their MLS data.

subject: Re: Product Demo Debrief - Dashboard and Reporting Features
body_summary: Marcus Thompson requests additional details on data analysis time reduction metrics
body: Hi Jessica, Thanks for the follow-up and the integration documentation. The dashboard demo was compelling, and I wanted to drill down on the specific metrics we discussed. You mentioned the 25 percent data analysis time reduction - I need to validate this against our actual MLS reporting volumes before I can justify this to leadership. Our current baseline is roughly 40 hours per month on manual data compilation and report generation across the team. If we can demonstrate a concrete 10-hour monthly reduction using your platform, that changes the conversation significantly. Could we schedule that technical deep-dive you mentioned? I'd also like to include Sarah in that discussion since she's tracking the broader ROI picture. The 50k annual investment needs to show clear payback within 12 months on our specific workflows, not generic benchmarks. Let me know what week works best for a working session where we can map your solution against our actual data flow and reporting cadence. Thanks, Marcus

subject: Re: Product Demo Debrief - Dashboard and Reporting Features
body_summary: Jessica provides benchmark data showing 25 to 35 percent time reduction for similar workflows
body: Marcus, thanks for the detailed breakdown on your current workflow. Forty hours monthly on MLS data compilation is exactly the kind of baseline we can work with. I appreciate you pushing for specifics rather than relying on industry benchmarks - that's the right approach. I've pulled analysis from three similar real estate tech operations with comparable MLS reporting volumes to yours. Two of them achieved 28 to 35 percent reductions within the first quarter, which translates to your 10-hour monthly target. The third hit 24 percent by month two after optimizing their reporting cadence. I'd like to propose a working session for next week where we map your actual data flow against our platform. We can simulate your current 40-hour process and show where the automation captures time. Sarah should absolutely be there since she's evaluating the ROI picture. This isn't about pitching features - it's about proving the math works for PropelView's specific workflows. Does Tuesday or Wednesday work better for you both? I can have our technical team ready to walk through your MLS volumes in detail. Jessica

subject: Technical Validation Preparation - Legacy MLS Integration
body_summary: Jessica schedules technical validation call for April 28 to address MLS integration requirements
body: Hi Jennifer, following up on our demo discussion last week - the feedback on the reporting dashboard was really positive, and I want to make sure we address your technical requirements head-on. I'd like to schedule a focused technical validation call for Tuesday, April 28th at 2 PM to walk through our MLS integration approach in detail. Since legacy system compatibility came up as a consideration, I want to show you exactly how we handle that integration and answer any questions about custom development needs upfront. Sarah will join us as well to ensure we're aligned across operations and technology. Do you have 45 minutes available then? If that time doesn't work, I'm flexible and can work around your calendar. Looking forward to diving deeper on this - I think once we clarify the technical path forward, we'll be in great shape to move into evaluation. Let me know what works best for you. Thanks, Jessica

subject: Re: Technical Validation Preparation - Legacy MLS Integration
body_summary: Jennifer raises concern about custom development costs for legacy MLS system integration
body: Hi Jessica, thanks for the detailed walk-through on the MLS integration approach. I appreciate you taking the time to address this upfront - it's been a key concern for our team given how tightly our legacy system is woven into our daily workflows. Before we commit to the technical validation call on Tuesday, I need to be direct about one thing: we're concerned about potential custom development costs that might not be budgeted for this project. Can you confirm whether your standard MLS integration handles our specific configuration without requiring custom work on our end? I want to make sure we're not looking at surprise costs down the road. If the standard integration does require some customization, what would that timeline and investment look like? I'm happy to jump on the call Tuesday at 2 PM if you can give me clarity on this first. Sarah and I both want to move forward, but we need to understand the full technical and financial picture before we proceed to the next phase. Let me know your thoughts. Thanks, Jennifer

title: Technical Validation - Legacy MLS Integration
transcript:
Michael Chen: Good morning Jennifer and Marcus. Thanks for making time today. As you know, we want to dive deep into how our platform integrates with your legacy MLS system. Jennifer, I know this has been a key technical concern for you. Let's start there.

Jennifer Walsh: Absolutely. Michael, we appreciate the detailed prep materials Jessica sent over. Our MLS system is fairly old - we're running a 2015 version with custom data mappings. Before we move forward, I need to understand the scope of integration work. Will this require significant custom development on our end?

Michael Chen: That's exactly what we should clarify today. Let me walk through our standard MLS integration approach. We've built connectors for most major MLS platforms released since 2010. Can you tell me more about your specific MLS version and any custom fields you're tracking?

Marcus Thompson: We have about forty custom fields layered on top of the base MLS schema. Things like internal property scoring metrics, agent performance data, and market zone classifications. Those are critical to our reporting workflows.

Jessica Ramirez: Marcus, those are important details. From what you're describing, I'd say roughly thirty of those forty fields fall within our standard integration mappings. For the remaining ten, we have two paths. One is our partner integration network - we work with MLS customization firms who handle bespoke mappings without our team needing to do custom development. The other is that those fields might not need to flow into the dashboard initially. You could prioritize which ones drive your most critical reports.

Jennifer Walsh: So you're saying we won't need to budget for your professional services team to write custom code?

Jessica Ramirez: Correct. Standard integration, no custom development required from InsightModal. Your team would handle any internal field validation, but the connector itself is plug-and-play.

Michael Chen: This gets us to something we heard in the demo debrief. Marcus, you mentioned needing proof that we reduce data analysis time by twenty-five percent. That number came up around your current manual reporting process, correct?

Marcus Thompson: Yes. Right now, pulling a market analysis report takes our team about four hours across three analysts. We query the MLS, extract into spreadsheets, reconcile the custom fields, and build Tableau visualizations. If your dashboard cuts that to three hours, that's meaningful efficiency.

Michael Chen: We can absolutely show you that with your actual data in a proof-of-concept environment. Here's what I'm proposing: we set up a small sandbox with a subset of your MLS data - maybe two market zones and a hundred properties. You walk through your standard analysis workflow in our dashboard. We time it against your current process. That gives you concrete evidence rather than estimates.

Jennifer Walsh: How long would that take to set up on your side?

Jessica Ramirez: Three to four business days. We'd need read-only access to your MLS staging environment and a list of those custom fields so we can verify the mappings are working correctly.

Marcus Thompson: I can get that to you by end of week. Is that timeline okay?

Michael Chen: Perfect. That sets us up to run your proof-of-concept right after we receive that information. Jennifer, does this technical approach feel like it addresses your integration concerns?

Jennifer Walsh: Yes, actually. I was worried we'd be stuck writing custom code or waiting months for integration. This seems more straightforward.

Michael Chen: Great. Once we complete the POC and Marcus validates the time savings, we'll have concrete data for your evaluation. That should help with the ROI conversation as well.
summary: Michael Chen and Jessica Ramirez walked Jennifer Walsh and Marcus Thompson through the technical integration approach for PropelView's legacy MLS system. The team confirmed that standard integration applies without requiring custom development from InsightModal, addressing a key technical concern. The group agreed to execute a proof-of-concept using PropelView's actual MLS data to validate the twenty-five percent data analysis time reduction claim that Marcus Thompson had previously raised as a requirement.
objections_raised: Integration with legacy MLS system may require custom development that we have not budgeted for
The reporting dashboard needs proof it will reduce data analysis time by at least 25 percent
next_steps: Marcus Thompson to provide read-only MLS staging environment access and custom field list by end of week
InsightModal to set up proof-of-concept sandbox environment within 3-4 business days
PropelView team to run actual analysis workflow in dashboard POC to validate 25 percent time reduction claim
Schedule follow-up call after POC completion to review results and discuss ROI implications

title: Deal Review - PropelView Analytics Sentiment Shift
transcript: David (Manager): Let's debrief on the Technical Validation call that just wrapped. Michael, Jessica, I'm seeing the sentiment shifted from concerned to neutral on this one. What happened in there? Michael (AE): That's actually good news. When Jessica walked through the legacy MLS integration capabilities, the buyer finally understood how our platform would work with their existing infrastructure. They were really worried about migration complexity before the demo. Jessica (SE): Right. I showed them the API bridge we built specifically for their system. They asked detailed technical questions about data mapping and latency, which to me signals engagement rather than hesitation. The neutral sentiment reflects that they moved from worry to genuine evaluation mode. David (Manager): So they're no longer concerned about technical fit. Michael (AE): Correct. The CTO even said the integration approach was cleaner than they expected. What shifted the needle is we addressed their biggest technical blocker head-on. David (Manager): And the budget conversation is coming up next week. Are we confident they'll stay engaged? Jessica (SE): I think so. They're asking for documentation on the integration timeline and implementation phases. That's a buying signal. Michael (AE): My sense is the neutral sentiment is actually a positive development here. They've moved from emotional concern to analytical evaluation. We just need to keep momentum and make sure the procurement discussion doesn't introduce new friction. David (Manager): Good. Let's make sure Jessica has all the technical documentation packaged before that procurement call. Michael, confirm the budget scope aligns with what they discussed today. Jessica (SE): I'll also prepare a technical integration summary they can share with their IT team. That gives them internal validation before we meet with procurement. David (Manager): Perfect. The move from concerned to neutral in Evaluation stage means we're progressing appropriately. Keep this pace going into Negotiation.
summary: PropelView Analytics shifted from concerned to neutral sentiment during technical validation, indicating the buyer moved past integration concerns and into analytical evaluation mode. Team agreed to maintain momentum by providing comprehensive technical documentation for the upcoming procurement discussion.
action_items: Michael to confirm budget scope alignment with previous buyer discussions
Jessica to prepare technical integration summary and timeline documentation for buyer IT team review
Michael to prepare procurement call with confidence that technical concerns have been resolved

note_preview: Technical validation call held. MLS legacy system integration discussed thoroughly.
author: Michael Chen
content: Held technical validation call with Jennifer Walsh today to dive deep into their legacy MLS system integration requirements. The integration path is more straightforward than initially anticipated and Jennifer indicated they have standard APIs available. Need to follow up with detailed documentation on our MLS connector to move this forward.

note_preview: Objection resolved: Custom development path identified with Jennifer Walsh support.
author: Michael Chen
content: Jennifer walked us through the MLS integration requirements and confirmed our standard connectors will handle their legacy system without any custom development needed. This was the technical blocker David kept bringing up, so resolving it removes a major ROI concern. Need to follow up with a formal integration spec document to keep the momentum going.

subject: Re: Technical Validation Preparation - Legacy MLS Integration
body_summary: Jessica confirms no custom development required and shares standard MLS integration documentation
body:
Jennifer, thanks so much for your directness on this - I really appreciate it. Based on our technical call yesterday with your team, I wanted to confirm what we discussed: our standard MLS integration absolutely handles your current configuration without requiring custom development on your end. Your legacy system setup actually aligns well with how we've built our integration layer for other mid-market real estate platforms.

I'm attaching the standard MLS integration documentation that outlines exactly how we connect to your existing workflows. The integration uses our pre-built connectors, so there are no surprise costs or extended timelines here.

What was helpful from yesterday's call was understanding your specific data refresh requirements and reporting needs - that actually made the technical path even clearer. Jennifer, I think you'll feel confident about the feasibility once you review the attached specs.

Let's absolutely keep Tuesday at 2 PM on the calendar. By then you'll have the documentation in hand, and we can walk through the implementation timeline and next steps. Sarah, looking forward to connecting with you both.

Thanks again for keeping us honest on the details.

Jessica

subject: Procurement Process and Budget Discussion - Next Steps
body_summary: Michael outlines procurement timeline and prepares for budget finalization discussion
body:
David, following our technical validation discussions with Jennifer and the team, I wanted to move forward on the procurement timeline for PropelView's implementation.

We've confirmed that the MLS legacy system integration will work seamlessly with your current setup, which addresses the technical concern from earlier. Now that we have full technical alignment, I'd like to walk through the procurement process and budget timeline with you.

Here's what I'm proposing:

1. Contract review by end of this week
2. Budget finalization discussion for the 50k annual investment
3. Implementation kickoff scheduled for June

I know budget cycles can be tight, and I want to ensure we structure this in a way that works for your finance planning. Could we schedule a 30-minute call next week to discuss the procurement steps and explore any flexibility around payment terms or contract start dates if needed?

Sarah and I are aligned on moving forward, and I believe this will deliver significant value for your data analysis workflows.

What works best for your calendar?

Best,
Michael

subject: Re: Procurement Process and Budget Discussion - Next Steps
body_summary: David informs that CFO has directed halt on new SaaS purchases until Q3 due to budget constraints
body: Michael, I appreciate you walking through the technical path forward. Jennifer confirmed the MLS integration approach will work without custom development, which is helpful for our planning. However, I need to flag a constraint on our end. Our CFO issued a directive this morning to halt all new SaaS purchases through Q3 as part of a broader budget reallocation initiative. This wasn't something we anticipated when we kicked off discovery with your team. I know this creates friction given the momentum we've built around the 50k annual investment and the June implementation timeline you proposed. Before we schedule that procurement call, I want to be transparent about where our budget sits. Rather than moving forward with a contract this quarter, we may need to revisit this conversation in July when Q3 planning clarifies. That said, I don't want to lose the technical alignment we've achieved. Could we explore whether there's a structure that addresses our budget freeze while keeping the deal viable for both sides? Sarah and I still see value here, but we need to work within the constraints we're facing. Let me know if you want to discuss options.

note_preview: Procurement and budget discussion call held with CFO David Kapoor present.
author: Michael Chen
content: Had procurement call with Sarah and David today. David outlined the CFO directive to halt SaaS purchases through Q3 due to budget reallocation, but he seemed open to discussing flexible payment terms that could bridge the gap. Sarah is clearly our champion here and pushed back on the blanket restriction, saying she sees the value in what we're proposing for their workflows.

title: Procurement and Budget Discussion
transcript: Michael Chen: Good afternoon, Sarah and David. Thanks for making time today. I know budget planning is critical right now, especially with the fiscal adjustments happening. I wanted to walk through how we can structure this engagement to work with your cash flow realities. Sarah Mitchell: Michael, we appreciate that. The technical side looks solid after our validation work with Jennifer. The MLS integration path is cleaner than we expected. But David and I need to align on the financial mechanics here. David Kapoor: Right. So Michael, I need to be direct. Our CFO issued a directive last week to halt all new SaaS commitments through Q3. We're reallocating budget to some enterprise infrastructure work. It's not about InsightModal specifically—it's a company-wide pause. Michael Chen: I hear you, David. That's actually a common constraint at this stage. The good news is we have flexibility on how we structure this. What if we moved the service start date to align with your Q3 availability? That way the procurement happens now, approval is locked in, but the billing and implementation kick off when you have budget clarity. Sarah Mitchell: That's interesting. So we'd essentially be contracting now but deferring the financial obligation? Michael Chen: Exactly. It protects your budget cycle and guarantees us a confirmed start. We've done this with other mid-market firms facing similar reallocations. The contract terms are set, there's no scope creep, and your team gets breathing room. David Kapoor: What would the payment structure look like? Monthly, or do you need it annual upfront? Michael Chen: We typically prefer annual, but given the constraints you're navigating, we could do a 50-50 split—half at Q3 kickoff, half at month six of service. That spreads the impact on your budget. Sarah Mitchell: And the contract would lock in the current pricing if we sign now? Michael Chen: Yes. Pricing is fixed for the full three-year term, regardless of when implementation begins. It actually protects you from any increases we might introduce next fiscal year. Sarah Mitchell: That's compelling for us. David, from my perspective as operations leader, the technical validation proved this reduces our reporting cycle by 28 percent based on our sample data. The ROI math works if we can get past the cash flow issue. This actually solves it. David Kapoor: I appreciate that, Sarah. My concern has always been justifying $50k annually without seeing those results. But if we're deferring spend to Q3 and we've validated the efficiency gains, I think this is workable. I'd need to confirm with our CFO that a deferred-start contract doesn't violate the halt directive, but I'm optimistic. Michael Chen: Perfect. And if it helps, I can provide a letter explaining the structure—that procurement and contracting happen now, service delivery begins in Q3. That's often useful for finance governance. David Kapoor: That would definitely help. Let's plan for me to run this by the CFO early next week. Sarah Mitchell: In the meantime, should we start mapping out the implementation timeline for June? Michael Chen: Absolutely. Jessica and I can work with Jennifer to draft a 90-day implementation roadmap. We can have that ready before your CFO decision, so you're not delayed on the technical side.
summary: Michael and the PropelView operations and finance leadership discussed structuring the $50k deal around David's CFO-mandated SaaS purchasing halt through Q3. Michael proposed moving the service start and billing to Q3 while locking in contract terms and pricing now—a solution that addresses David's budget constraint while securing the deal. Sarah confirmed the technical validation supports the investment, and David committed to getting CFO approval on the deferred-start structure.
objections_raised: CFO has directed halt on all new SaaS purchases until Q3 due to budget reallocation constraints
next_steps: Michael to provide letter explaining deferred-start contract structure for CFO review
David to present proposal to CFO early next week for approval
Jessica and Michael to develop 90-day implementation roadmap with Jennifer Walsh for technical planning
Team to reconvene after CFO decision to finalize contract terms

title: Deal Review - PropelView Analytics Budget Approval
transcript: Michael (AE): David, I wanted to get you and Jessica on a call right after the procurement discussion because the sentiment shifted again. We went from neutral to positive. This is significant momentum. David (Manager): Excellent. Walk me through what changed. Michael (AE): The budget conversation went smoother than expected. They came in prepared with allocated budget for analytics tools in their 2026 roadmap. When we presented the 50k ARR pricing, there was no pushback. In fact, the procurement director asked about implementation start dates and annual renewal terms. Jessica (SE): That's a buying signal. The questions shifted from whether they should buy to when they can start. Michael (AE): Exactly. The positive sentiment reflects that they've moved from analytical evaluation into buyer confidence. They see value and they have budget. David (Manager): Did they express any remaining concerns? Michael (AE): None that felt material. They asked about support tiers and customization options, but those are implementation questions, not deal questions. Jessica (SE): I noticed they asked to loop in their CFO for final approval. That's normal for a 50k deal, but it signals they want this. David (Manager): So we're moving toward contract review now. Michael (AE): Yes. They want to see the statement of work and master service agreement. We're looking at an executive alignment call next week to finalize terms. David (Manager): This is good progression, Michael. We went from concerned about technical fit, to neutral on validation, to positive on budget and procurement. Jessica (SE): Should we anticipate any questions on the executive call? Michael (AE): Probably just clarifications on SLAs, support response times, and any customization scope. Nothing that should derail this. David (Manager): Alright. Jessica, make sure the SOW is crisp and specific to their legacy MLS integration requirements. Michael, coordinate that executive alignment call and make sure we have all approvals lined up from our side before we meet with their CFO. Michael (AE): Will do. I'm confident we close this in the next two weeks.
summary: PropelView Analytics shifted from neutral to positive sentiment during procurement and budget discussion, indicating buyer confidence and budget approval. Team is now moving toward executive alignment and contract review with clear closing trajectory.
action_items: Jessica to prepare detailed statement of work with legacy MLS integration specifications and implementation timeline
Michael to schedule executive alignment call with CFO for final contract review within the next week
Michael to confirm all internal approvals and ensure sales team is aligned on final terms before CFO discussion

note_preview: Critical objection: CFO halting SaaS purchases through Q3 budget reallocation.
author: Michael Chen
content: David Kapoor just informed me that their CFO has imposed a company-wide halt on all new SaaS purchases through Q3 due to budget reallocation constraints. This is a hard stop regardless of our product fit or ROI story. We need to explore whether Sarah Mitchell can get an exception approved or if we need to pivot to a Q3 restart conversation.

subject: Re: Procurement Process and Budget Discussion - Next Steps
body_summary: Michael proposes flexible payment terms and extended contract start date to align with Q3 budget availability
body:
David, I appreciate you being direct about the CFO directive. Budget freezes are real constraints, and I respect the transparency. Here's what I'm thinking: we can structure this to work within your Q3 freeze while maintaining momentum.

What if we delay the contract execution to July 1st but lock in pricing and terms now? You'd have a signed agreement ready to activate the moment Q3 budget clarity arrives, with no additional negotiation needed. For the June timeline concern, we can frontload the technical onboarding and data mapping with your team at no cost during the waiting period so you're ready for a faster July launch.

Alternatively, we could explore a smaller pilot scope for Q2 under a different cost structure, then expand post-Q3 reallocation. Sarah's already a champion internally, and David, having you and Jennifer aligned on the technical path is exactly where we want to be.

I'd rather find a structure that works than lose momentum on something we all believe in. Can we jump on a quick call this week to discuss which approach feels right?

title: Final Executive Alignment and Contract Review
transcript: Michael Chen: Good morning everyone. Thanks for taking the time to align on this before we move to contract finalization. I know David flagged some budget constraints on the last call, so I wanted to make sure we address those head-on today. Sarah, Jennifer, David—thanks for being here. Let me start by walking through the flexible terms we discussed and see if they address the Q3 timing concern. Sarah Mitchell: Appreciate you jumping straight to it, Michael. We've been internal on this, and honestly the solution is strong. Our team ran some preliminary data through the reporting dashboards and the efficiency gains are real. I wanted David to hear directly that the business case is solid. David Kapoor: I appreciate that Sarah. Look, I'm not questioning the value. The technical fit is there, the ROI math works—we did our homework. But you know my position. The CFO directive came down mid-May. Any new SaaS commitments are on hold through Q3. It's not about this deal specifically. Jennifer Walsh: David, I get the budget pressure. But here's what we're looking at operationally. Our legacy MLS integration is becoming a bottleneck. Jessica's team confirmed it integrates without custom development, which saves us probably 30k in consulting costs. That was a real concern two weeks ago. Michael Chen: Exactly. And that's where we wanted to land with you today, David. We can't change the CFO directive, but we can change the timing structure. What if we extend the contract start date to Q3—July 1st—but lock in the pricing now at the current rate? That eliminates the approval risk for you, gives you the budget runway you need, and we get the deal off your plate. David Kapoor: That's more workable. But I still need to think about whether we can commit contractually to something that doesn't activate until July. Sarah Mitchell: From an operations standpoint, Michael, I'd rather not push this off. But if the finance structure solves David's problem and we get guaranteed pricing, that's a reasonable middle ground. We can use the next six weeks to finalize implementation planning anyway. Jennifer Walsh: Agreed. Plus we'd be ready to go live faster in July if we do the prep now. Michael Chen: So here's what I'm proposing. We execute a contract with a deferred start date of July 1st, 2026. You commit to the fifty thousand annual fee, locked at current pricing. We build an implementation plan in June and go live immediately at start date. That way your new fiscal year funding covers it, and we've eliminated the rework on the technical side. David Kapoor: The locked pricing matters. I don't want to commit and then hear we're renegotiating in June. Michael Chen: Absolutely. This is your price, full stop. And because we're extending the timeline, we can throw in ninety days of extended onboarding at no additional cost. Jennifer, that should give your team plenty of runway. Jennifer Walsh: That helps. Michael Chen: So David, does a deferred-start contract with locked pricing and extended onboarding work for you to take back to the CFO? David Kapoor: I need to confirm the exact legal structure works with procurement, but the commercial framework solves my main issue. Let me run it up. Sarah Mitchell: I'm comfortable moving forward on this basis. Michael Chen: Fantastic. So next steps—David, you confirm with procurement. Sarah, I'll send over the draft contract terms by end of day tomorrow. We target execution by end of week?
summary: Michael Chen proposed a deferred-start contract structure with July 1st activation and locked pricing to align with PropelView's Q3 budget constraints. Sarah Mitchell confirmed the business case remains strong and she supports moving forward. David Kapoor indicated the flexible timing resolves his primary CFO approval concern, pending procurement confirmation.
objections_raised: CFO has directed halt on new SaaS purchases until Q3 due to budget reallocation constraints
next_steps: David Kapoor to confirm deferred-start contract structure with procurement team
Michael Chen to send draft contract terms by May 22nd EOD
Target contract execution by end of week May 26th
Jennifer Walsh and Michael Chen to schedule implementation planning call for June

note_preview: Final executive alignment call completed. Contract review discussed final terms.
author: Michael Chen
content: Got all three stakeholders on the call this afternoon - Sarah, David, and Jennifer. We walked through the final contract terms and David confirmed they can work with our flexible payment schedule that pushes the first invoice to Q3 when their budget reallocation clears. Sarah's ready to sign and we're looking at implementation kickoff in early June pending legal review.

subject: Contract Review Complete - Executive Alignment Final
body_summary: Michael confirms final contract review and summarizes agreed terms including flexible payment schedule
body: Sarah and David, Thanks for the time this afternoon to align on final terms. I wanted to confirm we have a solid path forward that addresses both the technical requirements and budget constraints PropelView raised. Here is what we agreed to: InsightModal will implement our analytics platform with standard MLS integration at 50k ARR. Given the Q3 budget reallocation David mentioned, we have structured the contract with flexible payment terms - initial implementation begins in June with payment aligned to your budget cycle. This approach lets you move forward immediately on the reporting dashboard work Jennifer validated while managing your cash flow through Q3. The contract reflects our conversation and includes 90 days post-implementation support to ensure your team achieves the 25 percent reduction in data analysis time Marcus outlined. Jessica and I are ready to kick off implementation the week of June 3rd. Please review the attached final contract and let me know if you have any questions. We are excited to help PropelView unlock better insights from your real estate data. Michael

messages.body: Waiting on David's procurement confirmation. He said he'd push it through after CFO approval. Its been a week since I sent the formal proposal.
Have you reached out to check status? Sometimes these things stall without a nudge.
I sent a follow-up to David yesterday. No response yet. Sarah seemed committed when I talked to her but she's not the decision maker on the procurement side.
Want me to reach out to Marcus on the technical side? Sometimes the tech team can push internal procurement if they're happy with the solution.
That's smart. Marcus seemed on board after the POC. Can't hurt. David said CFO approved it but now he's mentioning budget reallocation priorities. I think this is starting to slip.
Budget reallocation is cover. The CFO freeze probably got extended. We might need to revisit the deal structure or accept this is a push to Q4.
Got the word back. David said they're pausing. CFO expanding the freeze into Q3. He said they still want it but timing is off. We're marked closed lost.
Did he give you any sense of Q4 timing? Are we staying warm with them or is this truly lost?
He said to stay in touch but their budget cycle is locked. Said reach out in late August for Q4 planning. So it's not a hard no, just - the deal isn't happening this fiscal year.
Got it. Mark it closed lost but keep them in the funnel. We'll pick it back up in August. Not ideal but at least the business case was solid.

subject: Re: Contract Review Complete - Executive Alignment Final
body_summary: Sarah confirms executive alignment and authorizes contract execution with InsightModal
body: Michael, Thank you for walking us through the solution one more time. After discussing internally with David and Jennifer, we are confident this is the right move for PropelView. The flexible payment structure you proposed genuinely solves our Q3 budget challenge while letting us move forward immediately on the reporting work. Jennifer confirmed the standard MLS integration will work seamlessly with our existing systems, and honestly, the promise of 25 percent efficiency gains on data analysis aligns perfectly with our operational goals. David has signed off on the procurement approach, and I am authorizing us to move forward with contract execution. I have reviewed the attached contract and everything reflects our conversation accurately. The June 3rd kickoff timeline works well for our team. We are excited to partner with InsightModal and get this implementation underway. Please send over any final documents we need to sign, and let us know what information you need from us to begin the onboarding process. Thanks again for being flexible on the terms and for your patience throughout our evaluation. Sarah

subject: Re: Contract Review Complete - Executive Alignment Final
body_summary: Michael confirms contract signed and outlines implementation kickoff schedule for June
body: Sarah, excellent news - I have the fully executed contract in hand as of this morning. Thank you for moving quickly through the final review with David and Jennifer. Your team's thoroughness throughout this process has been invaluable. We worked through some meaningful technical questions around the MLS integration, and I appreciate how you championed the value case internally despite the Q3 budget constraints. The flexible payment schedule we structured should give you the breathing room you needed while still getting started in June. Jessica and I are coordinating the implementation kickoff for the week of June 3rd. We'll send over the detailed onboarding timeline and resource requirements by end of day tomorrow. Our technical team is already pulling together the MLS connector documentation and will be ready for your initial setup call. This partnership has real potential to streamline your reporting workflows. Looking forward to showing you the impact on your data analysis efficiency over the next quarter. Let me know if you need anything before kickoff.

note_preview: Deal lost — CFO budget reallocation through Q3 prevented commitment.
author: Michael Chen
content: Despite strong support from Sarah Mitchell and positive momentum through negotiation, David Kapoor's Q3 budget freeze directive became a hard stop we couldn't overcome. We had the champion, solved the technical objections, and proposed flexible payment terms, but the CFO's company-wide SaaS spending halt was non-negotiable. Lost deal on May 27 due to budget constraints outside our control, but Sarah indicated they want to revisit in Q4 when budget becomes available again.
